name: Scott M. Lawrence
title: SVP & Chief Product Officer
aboutBio: SVP and Chief Product Officer for Verizon Business, responsible for the holistic product portfolio and strategy including Network Services, Platforms, Cyber Security, Connected Experiences, Fixed Wireless Access, and IoT Solutions. I oversee a global team that develops a clear and compelling vision for product-led growth across all of Verizon Business' customers. Results-driven technology executive with significant experience creating and maintaining high performance organisations across large enterprise sales, business strategy, process improvement and sales operations, specialising in global complex integration solutions. Throughout my career I have demonstrated a successful track record of developing trust-based relationships by aligning business outcomes with clients' digital transformation strategies, resulting in the proactive creation of measurable and sustainable client value.

articlesWritten.title: Investing in Career Development: How Verizon is Empowering V Teamers
articlesWritten.excerpt: Career progress is people's number one motivation to learn. At Verizon, we are investing in career development by refreshing practices and introducing new tools to expand our employees' options for future roles.

socialPosts.text: The signal I look for is never in the press release. At MWC this week, I had some of the most energising conversations I have had in years — about where AI in the network is actually going, not where vendors want you to think it is going. The gap between the headline and the reality is still large. But the reality is catching up fast.

We just announced the Verizon Business Assistant — a conversational generative AI tool for small businesses operating on SMS. This is not a chatbot. It is an on-ramp into an AI experience that learns your business, your hours, your menu, your processes. And it gets smarter over time. This is how you monetise AI — by bringing services to market that actually make a difference to how businesses operate day to day.

A Tale of Three Kings — Q3 US Wireless. The market has fractured into three clear strategies. Verizon plays the Yield Strategist, defending margin over volume. Q3 service revenue rose 2.1% to $21 billion. The question is not which strategy wins today — it is which one wins at the next inflection point. I know which one I am betting on.

Career development is one of the most important investments a company can make. We are seeing a remarkable increase in Verizon employees applying for and moving into new roles across the organisation since we transformed our approach — and 1 in 3 of those moves is to a completely different job family. That is not just mobility. That is talent strategy working.

volunteering: Best Buddies Virginia & DC — annual supporter
visionQuotes: The signal I look for is never in the press release.
This is how you monetise AI — by bringing services to market that actually make a difference to how businesses operate day to day.
strategies: Build a product-led growth organisation that develops compelling vision across the full Verizon Business portfolio
Commercialise AI through practical, revenue-generating products rather than proof-of-concept experimentation
Defend Verizon's margin position by competing on quality and outcome rather than price
Develop Connected Experiences and CX products that address the enterprise shift from on-premise to cloud-native contact centre
Expand Fixed Wireless Access as a business connectivity product alongside core mobile and fibre
challenges: Orchestrating a coherent product strategy across a diverse portfolio spanning network, security, CX, IoT, and AI
Competing with hyperscaler AI product investments from Microsoft, Google, and AWS that are reshaping enterprise expectations
Defending Verizon's premium network positioning while AT&T and T-Mobile compete aggressively on price
Moving fast enough on AI product development to match market expectations set by pure-play AI companies
Translating a complex B2B product portfolio into simple, compelling narratives for enterprise buyers
opportunities: Scale Verizon Business Assistant and generative AI product line across small and mid-size enterprise segments
Build Cybersecurity managed services into a leading managed security practice rivalling dedicated MSSPs
Expand Fixed Wireless Access addressable market as 5G coverage extends and reliability improves
Develop AI-native contact centre products to compete in the rapidly growing CCaaS market
Grow international product applicability leveraging EMEA leadership experience
trendsInsights: Generative AI is moving from experimentation to production in enterprise applications — product teams must move at AI speed
Network-as-a-service models are replacing traditional connectivity contracts — product strategy must reflect outcome-based pricing
Cybersecurity product consolidation is creating opportunities for platform-level managed security propositions
Fixed Wireless Access is emerging as a genuine enterprise connectivity alternative in mid-market segments
The boundary between telecom and technology company is disappearing — Verizon's product strategy must reflect both identities
competitorsPartners: AT&T Business, T-Mobile for Business (primary telecom competitors)
Microsoft, Google, AWS (AI and cloud product competitors/partners)
productsTechnologies: Verizon Business Assistant (generative AI on SMS)
targetMarkets: Small and mid-size business (AI and connectivity products)
Media, technology and financial services verticals
kpiMetrics: Verizon Business Group annual revenue approximately $30 billion
Verizon Business serves over 99% of Fortune 500 companies
Verizon Business Assistant launched March 2025 — first generative AI product on SMS for SMBs
Q3 2025 service revenue $21 billion, up 2.1% year-on-year
25+ year career at Verizon spanning sales, EMEA leadership, and product ownership
decisionInsights: Simplicity-oriented — consistently drives toward clarity and removing unnecessary complexity from strategy and execution
Signal-driven — looks beyond press releases to underlying market dynamics before forming views
AI-pragmatic — focuses on AI commercialisation through real products, not conceptual roadmaps
Outcome-aligned — track record of aligning technology investment with measurable customer and business results
Strong internal talent developer — vocal advocate for employee development and internal mobility
risksThreats: Hyperscaler AI product investments creating competitive pressure in enterprise technology spending decisions
Verizon's premium positioning vulnerable to price-led competition from AT&T and T-Mobile
Portfolio breadth creating execution risk — too many products competing for investment and attention
AI product development speed risk — enterprise expectations set by nimble AI companies are hard to match in a large organisation
Economic uncertainty reducing enterprise technology spend and extending product sales cycles
quotes: Think of this as kind of an on-ramp into an AI experience for small business. You want to get information on what is your business hours, what's your menu. And then it learns and it continues to build off that.
This is how you monetise AI — by bringing new services and applications to market that make a real difference.
bio: Scott M. Lawrence is SVP and Chief Product Officer for Verizon Business, responsible for the company's holistic product portfolio and strategy including Network Services, Platforms, Cyber Security, Connected Experiences, FWA, and IoT Solutions. He oversees a global product team driving product-led growth across Verizon Business' enterprise and public sector customers. His career at Verizon spans over 25 years, including roles as Group VP & GM for EMEA, VP of Advanced Solutions Sales & Engineering, and multiple enterprise sales leadership positions. He is a Fast Company Executive Board member and represented Verizon at Mobile World Congress 2026. He holds a BBA from the University of Wisconsin-Madison.
